summary: Soaring New Potato, monster Dark Star and power‑out drama
blurb: The show delivers a towering New Potato Caboose bass solo and a focused 20‑minute Dark Star that keeps the band locked in. A sudden power cut during Turn On Your Lovelight adds legendary urgency, while Dupree’s Diamond Blues features Pigpen’s harp. Overall the performance is tight, adventurous, and captured with excellent soundboard clarity.
review:
On January 24, 1969 at San Francisco’s Avalon Ballroom the Dead turned a one‑hour slot into an unforgettable night. After an unusually long tuning intro the first set unfolded through Cryptical Envelopment → The Other One → Cryptical Envelopment before launching into a soaring New Potato Caboose that showcases Phil’s earth‑shaking bass solo and Jerry’s Les Paul‑rich guitar tone, with Tom Constanten’s delicate keys weaving through. Dupree’s Diamond Blues shines thanks to Pigpen’s harp, and Doin’ That Rag keeps the groove rolling.

The second set erupts with a monster Dark Star that feels both exploratory and tightly focused, highlighted by Mickey’s maracas and the twin‑drum power of Billy and Mickey. The night takes an unexpected turn when management cuts power during Turn On Your Lovelight; the drummers keep the rhythm alive, turning a technical glitch into a piece of Dead lore. Miller’s remastered soundboard capture preserves every nuance, making this Avalon show a must‑listen for any 1969 collection.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All six recordings are uniformly positive, repeatedly praising the New Potato Caboose, Dark Star and the power‑out moment; descriptors include 'excellent', 'earth‑shaking', 'monster' and 'very good'. Consistent high praise across multiple sources warrants a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Miller’s remaster delivers excellent soundstage, crisp balance and captures the soaring New Potato solo and monster Dark Star with clarity.

key_highlights: Earth‑shaking New Potato Caboose bass solo that dominates the first set
A focused 20‑minute Dark Star showcasing tight improvisation
Power outage during Turn On Your Lovelight, with drums keeping the show alive
Pigpen’s harp adds a special flavor to Dupree’s Diamond Blues

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s Les Paul tone shines; his improvisational solo after New Potato is described as symphonic and beautiful, while vocals start low in the mix but come forward later.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh delivers a thunderous, earth‑shaking New Potato bass solo that many reviewers call entering the 'Phil Zone', anchoring the jams throughout.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals are initially undermixed but improve, delivering joyful leads on Dupree’s Diamond Blues and adding harmonies across the set.
band_performance.Tom: Constanten provides delicate, nuanced keyboards that complement The Other One and Dark Star, though a few reviewers note slight shakiness early in the show from the new keyboardist.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drumming is praised for its energy, including tasteful maracas on Dark Star and keeping rhythm alive during the power cut.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann delivers exceptional drum work, especially after the outage, driving the band forward with steady power.
